title: IELTS Reading — High-Frequency
description: Content and description vocabulary that appears again and again in IELTS Academic Reading passages — words for trends, evidence, comparison, and scientific explanation.

cards.definition: a fact or situation that is observed to exist, especially one whose cause is in question
cards.example: El Niño is a natural phenomenon that affects weather across the globe.
cards.quiz_prompt: El Niño is a natural ___ that affects weather across the globe.

cards.definition: a proposed explanation put forward as a starting point for further investigation
cards.example: Scientists tested the hypothesis that the drug slows the ageing process.
cards.quiz_prompt: Scientists tested the ___ that the drug slows the ageing process.

cards.definition: coming after something in time; following
cards.example: The initial findings were confirmed by subsequent experiments.
cards.quiz_prompt: The initial findings were confirmed by ___ experiments.

cards.definition: a gradual and continuous loss of strength, numbers, or quality
cards.example: The report documents a sharp decline in insect populations.
cards.quiz_prompt: The report documents a sharp ___ in insect populations.

cards.definition: to become known or to appear gradually
cards.example: New evidence has emerged that challenges the earlier theory.
cards.quiz_prompt: New evidence has ___ that challenges the earlier theory.

cards.definition: existing or available in large quantities; plentiful
cards.example: Fish were once abundant in these coastal waters.
cards.quiz_prompt: Fish were once ___ in these coastal waters.

cards.definition: next to or very near something else
cards.example: The laboratory is housed in a building adjacent to the library.

cards.definition: to publicly support or argue in favour of something
cards.example: The author advocates a complete reform of the education system.
cards.quiz_prompt: The author ___ a complete reform of the education system.

cards.definition: open to more than one interpretation; not having one clear meaning
cards.example: The wording of the law is ambiguous and frequently disputed.
cards.quiz_prompt: The wording of the law is ___ and frequently disputed.

cards.definition: to regard something as being caused by a particular thing
cards.example: Historians attribute the collapse to a combination of factors.
cards.quiz_prompt: Historians ___ the collapse to a combination of factors.

cards.definition: to make up for something undesirable by an opposite force or effect
cards.example: Some plants compensate for low light by growing larger leaves.
cards.quiz_prompt: Some plants ___ for low light by growing larger leaves.

cards.definition: giving rise to strong public disagreement
cards.example: The decision to build the dam remains highly controversial.
cards.quiz_prompt: The decision to build the dam remains highly ___.

cards.definition: to become progressively worse
cards.example: Air quality in the city has deteriorated over the past decade.
cards.quiz_prompt: Air quality in the city has ___ over the past decade.

cards.definition: to become or make less in size, importance, or intensity
cards.example: The supply of fresh water is steadily diminishing.
cards.quiz_prompt: The supply of fresh water is steadily ___.

cards.definition: showing a great deal of variety; very different from one another
cards.example: The rainforest supports an extraordinarily diverse range of species.
cards.quiz_prompt: The rainforest supports an extraordinarily ___ range of species.

cards.definition: most important, powerful, or noticeable
cards.example: English has become the dominant language of international science.
cards.quiz_prompt: English has become the ___ language of international science.

cards.definition: to improve the quality, value, or extent of something
cards.example: Regular exercise can enhance both memory and concentration.
cards.quiz_prompt: Regular exercise can ___ both memory and concentration.

cards.definition: to make use of a resource, sometimes unfairly for one's own advantage
cards.example: Early settlers exploited the region's rich mineral deposits.
cards.quiz_prompt: Early settlers ___ the region's rich mineral deposits.

cards.definition: to rise and fall irregularly in number or amount
cards.example: Prices fluctuated wildly during the economic crisis.
cards.quiz_prompt: Prices ___ wildly during the economic crisis.

cards.definition: a system in which people or things are ranked one above another
cards.example: Bees live in a strict social hierarchy.

cards.definition: existing as a natural and permanent part of something
cards.example: There are certain risks inherent in any scientific experiment.
cards.quiz_prompt: There are certain risks ___ in any scientific experiment.

cards.definition: a new method, idea, or product
cards.example: Technological innovation has transformed modern agriculture.
cards.quiz_prompt: Technological ___ has transformed modern agriculture.

cards.definition: very complicated or detailed
cards.example: The watch contains an intricate arrangement of tiny gears.

cards.definition: to make something less severe, serious, or harmful
cards.example: Planting trees can help to mitigate the effects of climate change.

cards.definition: new and original; not resembling anything known before
cards.example: The team developed a novel approach to treating the disease.

cards.definition: a statement or situation that seems contradictory but may nonetheless be true
cards.example: It is a paradox that the loudest critics are often the least informed.

cards.definition: to come or go before something in time or order
cards.example: A period of rapid growth preceded the sudden collapse.

cards.definition: important and well known; standing out so as to be easily seen
cards.example: She became a prominent figure in the environmental movement.

cards.definition: able to recover quickly from difficult conditions
cards.example: Coral reefs are surprisingly resilient to short-term stress.

cards.definition: to form an opinion about something without firm evidence
cards.example: Scientists can only speculate about what caused the mass extinction.

cards.definition: the level or point at which something begins to happen or take effect
cards.example: Pain is felt only once stimulation crosses a certain threshold.

cards.definition: found or distributed over a large area or among many people
cards.example: There is now widespread agreement on the main causes of the problem.
